Hail of Extraordinary Size; and Earthquakes in Bithynia and the Hellespont.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Hail of Extraordinary Size; and Earthquakes in Bithynia and the Hellespont.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On the 2d of June of the following year, in the consulate of Lupicin and Jovian, there fell at Constantinople hail of such a size as would fill a man’s hand.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Many affirmed that this hail had fallen as a consequence of the Divine displeasure, because of the emperor’s having banished several persons engaged in the sacred ministry, those, that is to say, who refused to communicate with Eudoxius.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: During the same consulate, on the 24th of August, the emperor Valentinian proclaimed his son Gratian Augustus.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: In the next year, when Valentinian and Valens were a second time consuls, there happened on the 11th of October, an earthquake in Bithynia which destroyed the city of Nicæa on the eleventh day of October.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This was about twelve years after Nicomedia had been visited by a similar catastrophe.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Soon afterwards the largest portion of Germa in the Hellespont was reduced to ruins by another earthquake.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Nevertheless no impression was made on the mind of either Eudoxius the Arian bishop, or the emperor Valens, by these occurrences; for they did not desist from their relentless persecution of those who dissented from them in matters of faith.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Meanwhile these convulsions of the earth were regarded as typical of the disturbances which agitated the churches: for many of the clerical body were sent into exile, as we have stated; Basil and Gregory alone, by a special dispensation of Divine Providence, being on account of their eminent piety exempted from this punishment.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The former of these individuals was bishop of Cæsarea in Cappadocia; while Gregory presided over Nazianzus, a little city in the vicinity of Cæsarea.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But we shall have occasion to mention both Basil and Gregory again in the course of our history.
